content.text: The sliding window technique is an algorithmic approach used to efficiently solve problems involving arrays or strings by maintaining a window of elements that can be resized as it moves through the data.

content.title: Key Point
content.text: Sliding window reduces time complexity from O(n²) or higher to O(n) by avoiding redundant calculations and processing each element at most twice.
Use sliding window when you need to find or compute something related to a subarray or substring, such as:
Finding maximum/minimum sum of subarray of length k
Finding longest substring with certain properties
Counting occurrences of specific patterns
Finding subarrays that meet specific criteria
In fixed size sliding window, the window maintains a constant size as it slides through the array. This is the simpler and more common variant.
Initialize the window with the first k elements
Compute the initial result (sum, count, etc.) for this window
Slide the window by adding the next element and removing the element that falls out
Update the result efficiently without recalculating everything
Repeat until the window reaches the end
Find the maximum sum of any subarray of size k in array [2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2] with k=3:
Initial window: [2,1,5] → sum = 8, max = 8
Result: Maximum sum is 9
Python implementation for maximum sum of subarray of size k:

content.title: Code Pattern
content.text: Always check edge cases like k > array length or empty arrays before implementing sliding window logic.

Variable size sliding window dynamically adjusts its size based on conditions. This is more complex and versatile, commonly used for problems with constraints like 'longest substring with at most k distinct characters'.
Variable size windows typically use two pointers (left and right) to define window boundaries:
Initialize left = 0, right = 0, and any auxiliary data structures
Expand window by moving right pointer, adding elements to current window
When condition is violated, shrink window by moving left pointer
Update the answer as you slide the window

subsections.title: Example: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters

content.text: Find the longest substring without repeating characters in 'abcabcbb':
Start: left=0, right=0, window='a', length=1
Expand: window='ab', then 'abc', length=3
Hit 'a' again: shrink left to skip first 'a', window='bca'
Continue: window='cab', then 'abc', length=3
Result: Longest length is 3 ('abc')
Sum/Product of subarrays of size k
Count distinct elements in every window
Maximum/minimum in each window
Anagrams within strings
Longest substring with at most k distinct characters
Minimum window substring containing all characters
Smallest subarray with sum at least k
Substring with all repeating characters

content.title: Important Facts
content.text: Choose appropriate data structures to optimize window operations. The right structure can simplify code and improve performance.
For fixed windows: Simple variables (sum, count, max, min)
For character counts: Hash maps/dictionaries
For order statistics: Monotonic queues, heaps
For set operations: Hash sets (for uniqueness)
With proper data structures, sliding window achieves O(n) time complexity:
Each element enters the window exactly once (right pointer)
Each element leaves the window exactly once (left pointer)
Data structure operations are O(1) or O(log k) amortized
Total: O(n) or O(n log k) depending on auxiliary structure

content.title: Critical
content.text: Always handle edge cases before implementing the main algorithm. Many bugs in sliding window problems come from unhandled boundary conditions.
Array or string is empty
Window size k exceeds array length
All elements are identical
No valid window exists
Single element array or string
Forgetting to remove elements when shrinking the window
Incorrectly updating auxiliary data structures
Using O(n²) or O(nlogn) operations inside the loop
Off-by-one errors in pointer movement
Not initializing variables properly
When encountering a new sliding window problem, follow this approach:
Identify if it's a fixed or variable size window problem
Determine what needs to be tracked (sum, count, characters)
Choose appropriate data structure for tracking
Define the condition for valid windows
Handle edge cases upfront
Implement and test with small examples

content.title: Debugging Strategy
content.text: Add print statements to track window boundaries (left, right positions) and the current window contents. This visualizes the sliding behavior and helps catch logic errors.
